fragments.content: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is defined as glucose intolerance first recognized during pregnancy, typically diagnosed in second or third trimester. Pathophysiology involves placental hormones (human placental lactogen, progesterone, cortisol) causing insulin resistance that exceeds maternal beta-cell compensatory capacity. Risk factors: obesity, advanced maternal age (>35), family history of diabetes, previous GDM, certain ethnicities (Hispanic, Asian, African American, Native American). GDM affects 6-9% of pregnancies. Complications include macrosomia, birth trauma, neonatal hypoglycemia, preeclampsia, cesarean delivery. Long-term risks: 50% develop T2DM within 10 years postpartum; offspring at increased risk for obesity and metabolic syndrome.

Management begins with medical nutrition therapy: carbohydrate distribution throughout day (minimum 175g/day), focus on low glycemic index foods, avoid excessive weight gain. Self-monitoring of blood glucose: fasting and 1-hour or 2-hour postprandial targets (fasting <95 mg/dL, 1-hour <140 mg/dL, 2-hour <120 mg/dL). If lifestyle modification fails to achieve targets after 1-2 weeks, pharmacotherapy initiated. Insulin is first-line: basal insulin (NPH or detemir) for fasting hyperglycemia; rapid-acting (lispro, aspart) for postprandial elevation. Metformin increasingly used as alternative but crosses placenta; glyburide less favored due to higher neonatal hypoglycemia risk. Postpartum: discontinue medications, screen for persistent diabetes at 6-12 weeks with 75g OGTT, then every 1-3 years.

Two screening approaches: One-step (IADPSG/WHO): 75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks; diagnose if any value meets/exceeds: fasting ≥92 mg/dL, 1-hour ≥180 mg/dL, 2-hour ≥153 mg/dL. Two-step (common in US): 50g glucose challenge test (non-fasting); if 1-hour ≥130-140 mg/dL, proceed to diagnostic 100g OGTT (Carpenter-Coustan criteria): diagnose if ≥2 values meet/exceed: fasting ≥95, 1-hour ≥180, 2-hour ≥155, 3-hour ≥140 mg/dL. Early screening (<24 weeks) recommended for high-risk women using standard diagnostic criteria. Universal screening recommended at 24-28 weeks. Postpartum screening essential: 75g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum, then every 1-3 years given high progression risk to T2DM.
